data.title: The US is headed for mass unemployment, and no one is prepared
data.description:
For years, I opposed Universal Basic Income, firmly and reflexively. I treated it as a liberal fantasy — an invitation to idleness, a subsidy for stagnation, a sedative administered by a bloated state. Work, I believed, wasn’t merely how societies functioned but how men and women found meaning. Pay people for nothing, and you dissolve discipline. That was the story. I told it often.

That position no longer survives contact with reality.

Something fundamental has shifted, and pretending otherwise is nothing short of denial. The AI revolution is here, and it’s gutting entire sectors with hurricane force. This isn’t an industrial transition, nor a replay of mechanization or globalization. It is a technological rupture of a different magnitude. Machines replacing not only muscle but cognition itself: judgment, pattern recognition, reasoning. And it’s advancing at a pace that outstrips legislation, labor markets, and political capacity, moving faster than most in government are willing to admit.

The most sobering warning comes from Geoffrey Hinton, one of the architects of modern AI. Hinton hasn’t joined the hype merchants. Instead, he has joined the alarmists. His claim is troubling: AI capability is effectively doubling every seven months. Not every decade. Not every few years. Every seven months.

At that pace, change doesn’t arrive gradually but in overwhelming waves. First, it replaces what we dismiss as “menial” cognitive work — call centers, customer service, scheduling, transcription. That phase is already underway. Then it moves into clerical roles, basic accounting, paralegal research, routine journalism, marketing copy, and compliance work. Those jobs are next. After that, no profession is spared, not even software engineering itself.

Hinton insists that within a few years, AI systems will complete month-long programming projects in hours. When that happens, junior developers will be removed rather than retrained. Teams will shrink. Entire layers will vanish. If the people who build the systems can be replaced by the systems, then no white-collar profession should feel insulated. 

Lay out the timeline honestly, and it becomes terrifying. In 2026, AI replaces support roles. In 2027, it consumes administrative and clerical work. By 2028, it’s performing serious professional tasks at scale. By the early 2030s, much of white-collar America may no longer be necessary to the current economic structure. 

This brings us to the politically radioactive part: the United States has no plan. None. No labor transition strategy. No reskilling conveyor belt capable of operating at this speed. No serious public conversation about income decoupled from employment. Just vague chatter about “innovation,” paired with the familiar promise that new jobs will somehow appear, as they always have.

We must dispense with the dangerous fiction and start facing the brutal reality.

A society where tens of millions are unemployable is not a sign of free-market success but a powder keg. You can’t preach personal responsibility to a population for whom responsibility has been rendered economically irrelevant. You can’t defend social order while ignoring the conditions that make order possible.

Universal basic income, viewed through this lens, stops looking like a left-wing indulgence and starts looking like a stability mechanism.

That doesn’t mean unconditional generosity or bureaucratic bloat. The conservative case for universal basic income is about preventing social fracture while preserving incentives to contribute, where contribution is still possible. It is about replacing a maze of failing welfare programs with something simple, transparent and limited.

Most importantly, it’s about buying time.

Universal basic income is not an end state but a bridge. A way to prevent mass dislocation while society renegotiates the relationship between work, dignity and income as the 9-to-5 day fades away.

I say this reluctantly, but honestly. Before AI, my opposition to universal basic income was rooted in a world that no longer exists. I assumed work would always be available for those willing to do it. That assumption is now obsolete. Not because people are lazy, but because machines are becoming capable faster than institutions can adapt.

The most dangerous response is to pretend this is a liberal argument, detached from objective reality. It is not. The social consequences of mass displacement — crime, despair, radicalization, resentment — spread. They destabilize everything conservatives claim to want to conserve.

We are approaching a moment where the question is no longer whether AI will replace jobs, but how a democratic society survives when it does. That conversation needs to begin now, while there is still time to shape policy deliberately rather than in panic. The country is already near a breaking point, marked by diminishing trust in institutions, the presidency and even one another. Some will argue that things could improve. They might, but it’s increasingly unlikely. For that reason, waiting is a luxury the country no longer has.

John Mac Ghlionn is a writer and researcher who explores culture, society and the impact of technology on daily life.

data.title: FBI raids Fulton Co. election center over 2020 race — move on!
data.description:
When you hear “FBI raid” and “elections facility” in the same sentence, it immediately hits a nerve in this country. Because for years now, the lie that the 2020 election was stolen has been used like a match near dry grass. And now, investigators with the Federal Bureau of Investigations have raided an elections facility in Atlanta, searching for materials related to the 2020 election.  

This happened at the Fulton County Election Hub in Union City, a place that has been at the center of election denial claims and part of that sweeping Georgia case against President Trump that ultimately fell apart and was dropped.  

But let’s be clear about what’s happening here: this isn’t about “election integrity.” This is about obsession. An obsession with the fact he lost and a refusal to move on.  

Because the truth is, the 2020 election was not stolen. Joe Biden won. States certified the results. Congress accepted the results. Biden was inaugurated in January 2021. Election security officials — including Republicans and people in Trump’s own administration — said the election was secure. And when fraud happened, it was isolated and did not change the outcome. In four years, Trump and his allies have produced no evidence of widespread fraud.  

And we cannot forget what this looked like in real time.  

There was that hour-long call on Jan. 2 with Georgia Secretary of State Brad Raffensperger, first reported by The Washington Post, where Trump recycled debunked claims about dead voters, ineligible voters, mystery ballots and rigged machines, and still Raffensperger insisted Georgia’s election was fair and accurate. Days later, Gabriel Sterling, a Republican and Georgia’s voting system implementation manager, publicly batted down multiple false claims.

Prosecutors alleged Trump called election officials in Georgia to “find” 11,780 votes, the exact number he needed to win the state, and that his team was planning to submit a slate of electors to try to give Trump the victory, while also pushing to access voting machine data.  

So when we see the FBI show up now with a court order to seize physical ballots, ballot images and voter rolls from Fulton County’s 2020 election, I think a lot of Americans are asking the same question: Why are we still here?  

“What should particularly concern us,” said Virginia Sen. Mark Warner, “is — guess who showed up at this FBI Raid? Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard. Tulsi Gabbard has nothing to do with election security. Tulsi Gabbard has even been in charged of dismantling what was called the Foreign Malign Influence Center, to try to make sure foreign countries don’t interfere in our elections. This complete Trump loyalist somehow popping up on an FBI raid, what the heck was she doing?”  

Even beyond the raid itself, it’s the pattern that stands out. This comes after the Department of Justice issued a complaint against Fulton County in December over 2020 election information. And it comes after DOJ attempts to investigate Trump’s perceived political enemies, including New York Attorney General Letitia James and former FBI Director James Comey, cases that were dismissed after a judge ruled Trump’s handpicked prosecutor was unlawfully appointed.  

U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff responded on X, and he didn’t mince words. “After losing Georgia in 2020, Donald Trump demanded state officials ‘find’ votes to change the outcome, tried to use DOJ to overturn it, and spread conspiracy theories that led to the Jan. 6 sacking of the U.S. Capitol.” 

He went on: “I suspect today’s raid is a continuation of this sore loser’s crusade, despite repeated audits and independent reviews confirming that Donald Trump was indeed defeated.”  

Georgia Democratic Sen. Raphael Warnock also released a statement. “The FBI should be focused on going after violent criminals, not carrying out political errands for a vengeful President. Republicans and Democrats agree: the 2020 elections were accurate and secure. End of story.”   

And that’s really the bottom line: the country cannot keep relitigating an election that was decided, certified, reviewed and confirmed. Look, people can be mad about losing. That’s politics. But we can’t keep rewriting reality every time someone doesn’t like the outcome. 

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.

data.title: Hillary Clinton: 'Savagery is a feature' of MAGA movement
data.description:
Former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton condemned the recent killing of Minnesotans by federal law enforcement officers, calling “savagery” a feature of the MAGA movement, in an op-ed in The Atlantic published Friday. 

“This crisis in Minneapolis reveals a deep moral rot at the heart of Trump’s movement,” Clinton wrote, adding, “‘The cruelty is the point,’ as The Atlantic’s Adam Serwer memorably put it during Trump’s first term. The savagery is a feature, not a bug.”

Clinton’s essay was largely a response to federal agents killing 37-year-old Alex Pretti on Saturday, just days after shooting Renee Good. She also condemned ICE’s handling of children. Federal agents detained a five-year-old in Minnesota, fueling anger toward the agency.

“Whatever you think about immigration policy, how can a person of conscience justify the lack of compassion and empathy for the victims in Minnesota, and for the families torn apart or hiding in fear, for the children separated from their parents or afraid to go to school?” Clinton wrote.

The Trump administration has taken a more conciliatory tone this week in its approach to immigration law enforcement after facing bipartisan pushback from the Minnesota killings, with Trump holding what he described as a “very good call” with Gov. Tim Walz (D) on Monday and sending border czar Tom Homan to replace firebrand Border Patrol commander Greg Bovino leading the Minneapolis operation.

“Governor Tim Walz called me with the request to work together with respect to Minnesota. It was a very good call, and we, actually, seemed to be on a similar wavelength,” Trump said in a post on Truth Social on Monday.

While the MAGA movement is closely aligned with the Christian right, Clinton argued in her piece that MAGA’s thinking and actions have gone against Christian values like “love thy neighbor.” She argued that the killings in Minnesota and nationwide show a lack of compassion and empathy by the Trump administration and the use of cruelty as a mechanism of fear. 

“That compassion is weak and cruelty is strong has become an article of MAGA faith,” Clinton wrote. “Trump and his allies believe that the more inhumane the treatment, the more likely it is to spread fear.”

Clinton said the “glorification of cruelty” and “rejection of compassion” reflect Trump’s own “character” and “worldview.” She argued that this has led to a movement of Christian influencers “waging a war on empathy” and threatening democracy in the U.S.

Clinton warned about the dangers of Christian nationalism — an ideology that seeks to fuse church and state into one. She pointed to the “Appeal to Heaven” flag — displayed in Speaker Mike Johnson’s window and carried on Jan. 6, 2021, by insurrectionists — as evidence that Christian nationalism is “ascendant in Trump’s Washington.” 

“[Christian influencers] twisted campaign validates Trump’s personal immorality and his administration’s cruelty,” Clinton wrote. “It marginalizes mainstream religious leaders who espouse traditional values that conflict with Trump’s behavior and agenda. And it threatens to pave the way for an extreme vision of Christian nationalism that seeks to replace democracy with theocracy in America.”

Clinton criticized social media for helping promote these ideas, arguing platforms like TikTok and X promote extremism over moderation.

“The medium is designed to boost vitriol and knee-jerk reactions rather than thoughtful dialogue,” Clinton wrote. “It provides fertile ground for misinformation and is inhospitable to serious journalism or debate.”

Clinton closed her op-ed with a call to action for people to follow the example of faith leaders, like the more than 100 clergy who were arrested for protesting deportation flights at the Minneapolis Airport last Friday. 

She said the dozens of liberal clergy who have signed up to run in the 2026 midterms is also an “encouraging sign.”

“To rekindle our light, we must reject cruelty and corruption,” Clinton wrote. “To be strong, we need more empathy, not less.”

data.title: Flying next week? This mistake could cost you $45 under a new TSA policy
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Planning to fly next week without your REAL ID? Be prepared to pay a hefty fee.

Starting on Feb. 1, 2026, travelers who fail to provide their REAL ID-compliant driver’s licenses or certain other acceptable forms of identification at a TSA security checkpoint will be subject to a $45 fee, the Transportation Security Administration has announced.

The TSA says its new policy will help cover the cost of its new TSA ConfirmID process, described as a “modernized alternative identity verification option” for travelers who forget to bring an acceptable form of ID or haven’t yet obtained one.

“Impacted travelers will have the option to pay $45 and use the TSA ConfirmID process,” Adam Stahl, the senior official performing the duties of deputy administrator for the TSA, said of the policy. “This fee ensures that non-compliant travelers, not taxpayers, cover the cost of processing travelers without acceptable IDs.”

The TSA’s REAL ID requirement went into effect in May 2025. Travelers flying without REAL ID in the months since have been instructed to bring an alternate acceptable form of identification, such as a passport or enhanced driver’s license.

“If your identity cannot be verified, you will not be allowed to enter the screening checkpoint,” the TSA warned.

Starting on Feb. 1, travelers who don’t bring an acceptable form of ID to a TSA checkpoint have the option of verifying their identity with TSA ConfirmID. Passengers should be prepared to give their legal name, address and date of birth during the process, which could take up to half an hour or more, according to the TSA.

After completing the TSA ConfirmID process and paying the fee, travelers will receive a receipt to show at the TSA checkpoint. Once there, they could undergo “additional ID verification, screening measures and potential delays,” TSA warned.

“If you choose not to use [TSA ConfirmID] and don’t have an acceptable ID, you may not be allowed through security and could miss your flight,” the agency writes.

Identity verification through TSA ConfirmID will be good for 10 days, according to TSA. As long as your return flight is within that 10-day timeframe, you should not need to go through the TSA ConfirmID process more than once.

Administrators with the TSA are, however, urging travelers to bring a REAL ID or other acceptable ID to the airport, or to pay the $45 fee ahead of time to reduce potential hiccups with travel. The TSA reported in December 2025 that 94% of travelers had been in compliance.

It’s also possible that many Americans already are REAL ID-compliant and don’t realize: The designation is marked by a star on a driver’s license or state ID card. The star may be black or gold, completely filled in, or only an outline.

Some states — Minnesota, Michigan, New York and Vermont —  issue REAL ID-compliant IDs and enhanced driver’s licenses (Washington only issues the latter). State-enhanced driver’s licenses are marked with a flag rather than a star and include a chip that can make it easier to enter the U.S. by land or sea from Canada, Mexico or the Caribbean, according to U.S. Customs and Border Patrol. (REAL IDs cannot be used for crossing the borders.)

In place of a REAL ID, TSA will accept multiple other forms of identification, including a passport, a Department of Defense ID (including those issued to dependents), and photo IDs issued by a federally recognized Tribal Nation/Indian Tribe, including Enhanced Tribal Cards.

  • Foreign government-issued passport
  • Veteran Health Identification Card
  • DHS trusted traveler cards
  • Permanent resident card
  • Border crossing card
  • HSPD-12 PIV cards
  • Canadian provincial driver’s license or Indian and Northern Affairs Canada card
  • Transportation worker identification credential
  • U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services Employment Authorization Card (I-766)
  • U.S. Merchant Mariner Credential

Expired IDs may also be acceptable up to two years past their expiration date.

“To avoid delays or missed flights, all travelers should obtain a REAL ID or another acceptable form of identification before heading to the airport,” Stahl said.

data.title: White House touts Don Lemon arrest
data.description:
The White House is standing firmly behind its decision on Friday to arrest former CNN anchor Don Lemon. 

“When life gives you lemons…” the White House account posted on the social platform X on Friday with a chains emoji. The text accompanied a photo of Lemon during his recent coverage of a protest inside a St. Paul, Minn., church. 

Lemon said he was at the protest in the capacity of a journalist.  

The Trump administration has sought to detain eight people, including Lemon, for their role at the protest against the federal immigration crackdown in the state. Demonstrators believed the church’s pastor was an Immigration and Customs Enforcement officer. 

The Justice Department says protesters violated the FACE Act — a law that protects people from attacks in places of worship. 

Trump responded to the protest in a Truth Social post, saying the protesters were “professionals.”

“They are highly trained to scream, rant, and rave, like lunatics, in a certain manner, just like they are doing,” Trump said. “They are troublemakers who should be thrown in jail, or thrown out of the Country.”

White House Deputy Chief of Staff James Blair noted in an X post on Friday that a federal jury had indicted Lemon prior to his detainment, saying, “He was not just magically arrested.” 

The Thursday grand jury indictment follows a decision by a Minnesota magistrate judge last week to reject the federal charges against Lemon related to the Minnesota church protest. 

Federal officials detained Lemon while he was covering the Grammy Awards in Los Angeles on Thursday evening, his lawyer said in a statement. 

Lowell said Lemon will challenge the charges “vigorously and thoroughly in court.” 

“The First Amendment exists to protect journalists whose role it is to shine light on the truth and hold those in power accountable,” Lowell said.

data.title: RFK Jr. on adding Trump's name to Kennedy Center: 'I've got bigger fish to fry'
data.description:
Robert F. Kennedy, Jr. is speaking out about President Trump’s name being added to the Kennedy Center, saying there are “more important” things than “a name on a building.”

“I was here when the Center was built,” the Health and Human Services secretary told ITK on Thursday, when asked about the Kennedy Center’s board voting last month to rename it “The Donald J. Trump and the John F. Kennedy Memorial Center for the Performing Arts” to honor the current and 35th presidents.

“The people who were involved in making this happen, they wanted to build it because of President [John F.] Kennedy’s commitment to… projecting American culture around the world — it was, my uncle believed, our great export,” the late president’s nephew said of the intention behind the creation of the performing arts institution, built in 1971 as a living tribute to Kennedy.

“President Trump feels the same way,” said the 72-year-old son of former Attorney General and Sen. Robert F. Kennedy (D-N.Y.) when ITK caught up with him at the premiere of “Melania,” held at the Kennedy Center.

Praising the commander in chief, Kennedy said, “He’s put tremendous energy thinking about this center, making sure that the building is beautiful.”

Trump, in an unprecedented move last year, overhauled the Kennedy Center’s board. After accusing it of being too “woke,” he replaced board members and named himself its chairman. He’s since toured the arts hub several times, vowing to beautify the space.

“We’re going to fix it up, but it’s really emblematic of our country,” Trump said in March. 

While the Center’s interim President Richard Grenell said that the “decision of the Trump Kennedy Center Board doesn’t impact the Memorial to Kennedy set up by Congress,” several members of the Kennedy family harshly condemned the move.

Kerry Kennedy, president of the Robert & Ethel Kennedy Human Rights Center and the younger sister of Robert F. Kennedy Jr., said in December, “President Kennedy proudly stood for justice, peace, equality, dignity, diversity, and compassion for those who suffer.”

“President Trump stands in opposition to these values, and his name should not be placed alongside President Kennedy’s,” she said. 

Maria Shriver, the daughter of former President Kennedy’s sister, Eunice Kennedy Shriver, expressed “disbelief” at the time after hearing of the name change. 

But Robert F. Kennedy Jr. indicated his focus was elsewhere.

“If I can save one kid’s life, it’s more important than a name on a building,” he said. 

“For me, I’ve got bigger fish to fry.”

data.title: Congress has the chance to help save the US shrimping industry
data.description:
In 2000, Texas was home to more than 2,500 licensed commercial shrimpers. Today, it is home to less than 1,000. According to the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Fisheries for the Gulf of America, there are only 1,467 federally permitted vessels in the entire Gulf of America.

Domestic shrimp contributed to approximately 50 percent of U.S. consumption in the 1980s. Now, imported, foreign shrimp accounts for more than 90 percent of the shrimp consumed in the United States, with domestic shrimp accounting for just 4 percent -10 percent of consumption.

You may be wondering what happened to the nation’s once prosperous shrimping industry. In 1980, the United States imported approximately 250 million pounds of shrimp. In 2024, the U.S. imported around 1.67 billion pounds of shrimp, with most of it coming from India, Ecuador and Indonesia. It is widely known that shrimp imported into our country are not wild-caught — they are shrimp grown on farms or hatcheries that use child labor and are ridden with dangerous working conditions.

Since the 1970s, multilateral institutions such as the World Bank and the United Nations have provided hundreds of millions of dollars in support of foreign shrimp operations in Asia and Latin America, including Ecuador, India and Belize, with Ecuador receiving the most financial aid.

In fact, between 1980 through 2000, the World Bank contributed over $950 million in loans to support shrimp aquaculture in Ecuador. The United States has executive directors representing our country and domestic industries at these multilateral institutions and is the most significant funder of these organizations. U.S. law requires U.S. executive directors at international financial institutions to vote against funding any project to produce or extract any commodity for export that would cause harm to U.S. producers of the same commodity or if there is a global surplus of that commodity.

Unfortunately, U.S. executive directors at these international financial institutions have historically disregarded this statute, which is intended to protect domestic producers like America’s shrimpers.

However, in April 2025, for the first time in history, the U.S. voted against funding a shrimp aquaculture project at an international financial institution.

While I appreciate the U.S. Treasury Department finally standing up for American shrimpers by voting against an Asian Development Bank project to fund $150 million in Thai shrimp production, this “no” vote was long overdue. For far too long, American tax dollars have been bank rolling foreign shrimp production, to the demise of our own shrimping industry.

Enough is enough.

For the last two congresses, I have proudly introduced the Save Our Shrimpers Act, legislation which would prohibit federal funds from being made available to international financial institutions to finance any activity relating to shrimp farming, shrimp processing, or the export of shrimp from any foreign country to the U.S. My legislation would also trigger a Government Accountability Office investigation and require annual reports to Congress outlining to what extent U.S. representatives at international financial institutions comply with existing law.

We need to completely turn off the money tap that has been using hard-working American tax dollars to bankroll foreign shrimp production while America’s shrimpers are struggling to stay afloat.

It is imperative that Congress passes my bill, the Save Our Shrimpers Act, expeditiously so that American shrimpers have the chance to survive. We need to codify my bill into permanent law, so that American shrimpers are protected, regardless of who occupies the White House.

I’m calling on Congress to do the right thing and to move my America First legislation so that American shrimpers can continue to do what they love for generations to come.

Troy Nehls represents Texas’s 22nd District in the U.S. House of Representatives.

data.title: Independent journalist Georgia Fort records moments before arrest over Minnesota church disruption
data.description:
“Agents are at my door,” independent journalist Georgia Fort told followers during a live stream on Facebook early Friday morning.

The founder of BLCK Press, a self-described social enterprise newsroom in Minnesota, was filming the moments before she surrendered to federal authorities on unspecified charges related to a disruption during a St. Paul church service earlier this month.

“You guys, I wanted to alert the public that agents are at my door right now,” Fort said at the start of the two-and-a-half-minute live stream.

Fort said that she was advised by her attorneys to “go with them…down to Whipple,” referencing the federal building in Hennepin County where protests against the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown have grown in recent weeks.  

Someone could be heard crying in the background as Fort noted her children were inside the home.

“This is all stemming from the fact that I filmed a protest as a member of the media. We are supposed to have our constitutional right of freedom to film, to be a member of the press. I don’t feel like I have First Amendment right as a member of the press because now federal agents are at my door, arresting me for filming the church protest a few weeks ago,” Fort said.

Fort was taken into custody alongside three others, including former CNN anchor Don Lemon, in connection with what Attorney General Pam Bondi described as a “coordinated attack on Cities Church in St. Paul, Minnesota.”

It remains unclear what specific charges the individuals could face, though Justice Department officials had previously indicated possible prosecutions under the FACE Act, a law that protects access to houses of worship “free of force or threats of force.”

The arrests come weeks after demonstrators entered a church where one of the pastors was believed to be affiliated with Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), interrupting a service with chants of “ICE out!”

Several independent journalists, including Fort and Lemon, were on the premises and documented the incident, which drew the ire of top Trump administration officials.

“You’re not allowed to stop people from worshiping, and you’re not allowed to intimidate or impede them. And so, the individuals that went in there and did that — and you can tell that they had a plan,” Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche said during a Fox News appearance.

“You can tell they had an agenda. You cannot do that in this country, and we’re investigating it, and I expect we will move very aggressively and very quickly to find the people that did this,” he added.

Both Fort and Lemon have argued they were there not as participants but to provide news coverage.

Fort told viewers on Friday that she knew she was on a “defendant’s list” and had been following the advice of her attorneys, reiterating that she felt her constitutional rights were being infringed upon.

She then walked to the front door, noting that agents were “knocking,” as another person is heard saying: “she’s coming right now.”

The video cuts off as Fort reaches to turn the doorknob.

data.title: Klobuchar says Trump making US 'less safe'
data.description:
Sen. Amy Klobuchar (D-Minn.) said President Trump is endangering American citizens amid his administration’s immigration crackdown in the state. 

Klobuchar, who jumped into the Minnesota governor’s race on Thursday, told MS NOW’s Lawrence O’Donnell that voters want to live in “places that are safe.”

“[Trump] is making them less safe,” she told the host. “The three first fatalities in the city of Minneapolis — three so far this year — two have been committed by federal agents.”

Immigration enforcement officials shot and killed resident Renee Good and Veterans Affairs ICU nurse Alex Pretti in separate instances in Minneapolis earlier this month. Their deaths have caused Democrats, and some Republicans, to question the Department of Homeland Security’s (DHS) use of force in carrying out the president’s deportation agenda.

Trump administration officials have defended the federal agents and maintained that the officers acted in self-defense. The incidents have also ramped up scrutiny of Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem and many lawmakers have called for the DHS chief to be held accountable.

Klobuchar on Thursday noted that she spoke to Pretti’s parents after he was killed last weekend, who said they wanted his “story told.”

“They described him as the kind-hearted man that he was, but they also said the hardest thing for them 24 hours later was what the officials in the administration, the Trump administration,” the Minnesota Democrat added.

On the day Pretti was killed, White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller called the 37-year-old protester a “domestic terrorist” and “would-be assassin” in separate posts on the social platform X. 

The president doubled down on his criticism of Pretti on Friday after a video circulated earlier this week appearing to show the late protester involved in a separate clash with federal agents prior to his death. He also rebuffed claims from border czar Tom Homan suggesting the administration would relax immigration operations in Minneapolis.

Klobuchar also expounded on her decision to jump into the North Star State’s gubernatorial race to replace outgoing Gov. Tim Walz (D). Walz announced earlier this month that he would not seek reelection amid a probe into alleged fraud involving the state’s social services programs.

The senator said the decision to run felt like something she “had to do” amid tensions in her home state. 

“I love my job, but I love my state more,” Klobuchar added. “And our state has been the center of America’s heartbreak, but we’ve also been the center of America’s hope and courage.”

data.title: DOJ releases final tranche of Epstein documents
data.description:
The Justice Department said Friday they have completed their review of the Epstein files, releasing 3 million additional documents to the public.

Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche told reporters Friday that the tranche being released will be one of the last steps required under a bill passed by Congress and signed into law by President Trump requiring the publication of the files related to deceased sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

“Today’s release marks the end of a very comprehensive documentation – document identification and review process to ensure transparency to the American people and compliance with the act. The department has engaged in an unprecedented and extensive effort to do so, after submitting the final report to Congress, as required under the Act, and publishing the written justifications for redactions in the Federal Register, the department’s obligations under the under the act will be will be completed,” Blanche said.

The deputy attorney general said he did not expect the release to quell interest in the case or end speculation about its contents but added that the Justice Department has not refused to charge any one who may have had sexual relations with or trafficked victims.

“There’s a hunger or a thirst for information that I do not think will be satisfied by the review of these documents and…there’s nothing I can do about that,” Blanche said.

“There’s this built in assumption that somehow there’s this hidden tranche of information of men that we know about, that we’re covering up, or that we’re choosing not to prosecute. That is not the case. I don’t know whether there are men out there that abuse these women. If we learn about information and evidence that allows us to prosecute them, you better believe we will,” he added.

“But I don’t think that the public or you all are going to uncover men within the Epstein files that abuse women, unfortunately.”
